pipeline_verified.cargo_doc_workspace_no_deps: green (default lint profile; see g7-cr-2 for missing_docs)

summary: G7 lands a coherent orchestrator surface. CRUD, registration, privileged system-zone API, transaction with commit-time cap hook, and change-broadcast wiring all compile clean and pass the pipeline gates. Single-file lib.rs (1789 lines) is navigable because sections are marked with prominent banner comments, but is at the upper end of 'acceptable' and should split before G8 adds napi-facing helpers. Test edits in compromises_regression.rs are genuinely surgical — compromise_4 tightens the grep per its own stated contract (was false-positiving on determinism.yml's legitimate cargo-build canary) and compromise_6 fixes a path-resolution bug (integration tests run from the crate dir, not repo root) mirroring an existing pattern in compromise_4. Neither weakens contract; both are defensible test-authoring fixes. Major issues: (1) production().capability_policy(X).without_caps() silently drops the configured policy with no error; (2) ~74 pub items lack doc comments (workspace lints don't enforce missing_docs, but the brief asks for it); (3) Box<dyn CapabilityPolicy> in builder surface will fight napi serialization in G8 — needs a policy-selector enum wrapper.

findings.claim: Builder footgun: production() + capability_policy(X) + without_caps() silently drops the configured policy without error.
findings.detail: The production guard at line 974 only checks `self.production && self.policy.is_none()`. A caller that writes `builder().production().capability_policy(custom_policy).without_caps().build()` passes the guard (policy is Some) but at line 1034 the `caps_enabled = !without_caps` branch sets `policy = None`, throwing away the explicitly-configured custom policy. Worse: the inverse case `production().without_caps()` (no explicit policy) fails with NoCapabilityPolicyConfigured even though the user signaled they don't want caps. The production guard and the without_caps switch are mutually inconsistent. This violates the R1-SC2 'fail-early' contract the production() flag is supposed to enforce.
findings.suggested_fix: Add a second guard in build(): if production && without_caps, return a distinct EngineError variant (e.g., ProductionRequiresCapabilities) explaining the contract. If production && !without_caps && policy.is_none(), the existing NoCapabilityPolicyConfigured fires. Document on without_caps() and production() that they are mutually exclusive. Alternatively: production() could implicitly disable without_caps, but an explicit error is safer.

findings.detail: Running `RUSTDOCFLAGS="-D missing_docs" cargo doc -p benten-engine --no-deps` produces 74 errors. The workspace lint config does NOT enable `missing_docs` so the default `cargo doc` passes clean, but the review brief explicitly asks 'every pub item has a doc comment'. Concrete undocumented surfaces: (a) EngineError variants Core/Graph/Cap (lines 57-64) — the #[error] attr provides the Display, not a rustdoc description; (b) Engine methods call_as, call_with_revocation_at, trace, handler_to_mermaid, handler_predecessors (lines 465-510) have no doc comments, only `Err(NotImplemented)` bodies; (c) RevokeSubject trait + as_value method + RevokeScope trait + as_scope_string method (1575-1605); (d) pub struct fields on Outcome's public siblings (TraceStep.duration_us etc.) — TraceStep has pub methods but several sibling types expose fields directly; (e) testing::CountingPolicy::call_counter, testing::CallCounter::load, testing::CountingPolicy struct.
findings.suggested_fix: One-pass docstring sweep before G8. For the NotImplemented stubs, a one-liner like `/// Phase 2: call_as — see call() doc.` suffices. Either enable `#![deny(missing_docs)]` on this crate to enforce going forward, or add a workspace-level `missing_docs = "warn"` in a G7 follow-up.

findings.claim: `capability_policy(Box<dyn CapabilityPolicy>)` in the public builder surface will not cross the napi v3 boundary cleanly.
findings.detail: The G8 brief ('~20 methods ready for napi bindings') implies `EngineBuilder::capability_policy` is one of them. Trait objects cannot be serialized by napi — napi-rs wants either concrete types, JS-side callbacks (via ThreadsafeFunction), or an enum/tagged-union of known policy backends. The public API commits us to a shape that G8 cannot wire without a TS-side adapter layer. Today: NoAuth is the only real backend; future: UCAN in Phase 3. A policy-selector enum (`PolicyKind::NoAuth | PolicyKind::Ucan { ... } | PolicyKind::Custom(Box<...>)`) would give napi a clear target while keeping the native Custom escape hatch.
findings.suggested_fix: Before G8 dispatches: add `pub fn capability_policy_no_auth(self) -> Self` and (post-Phase-3) `pub fn capability_policy_ucan(self, cfg: UcanConfig) -> Self` as the napi-facing surface. Keep `capability_policy(Box<dyn ...>)` for native-only custom backends. Gate the Box<dyn> variant behind `#[cfg(not(target_arch = "wasm32"))]` if wasm32 is a napi target.

findings.claim: Three `pub fn` items that are clearly test-only leak into the public release surface.
findings.detail: `Engine::test_subscribe_all_change_events` (606), `Engine::test_subscribe_change_events_matching_label` (611), and `Engine::testing_insert_privileged_fixture` (837) are documented as 'test-only' / 'fixture' but carry no `#[cfg(test)]` or `#[cfg(feature = "testing")]` gate. They are available in release builds published to crates.io. testing_insert_privileged_fixture additionally contains an `.expect("fixture insertion via NoAuth backend")` which is the ONLY expect() in production code (line 842).
findings.suggested_fix: Either (a) move these three methods into the existing `pub mod testing` module (consistent with the other testing helpers below), or (b) put them behind a `#[cfg(any(test, feature = "test-helpers"))]` gate and add a `test-helpers` feature to Cargo.toml. The public `subscribe_change_events` + `test_subscribe_change_events_matching_label` pair is particularly awkward — the label-filtered variant could be exposed via a parameter on subscribe_change_events so the test_ naming goes away.

findings.detail: If the caller passes old_cid and new_node with identical content-hash (no actual change), the transaction delete_node(old_cid) removes it, then put_node inserts identical bytes back. Inside one transaction this is ACID-safe but wastes work and, more importantly, is semantically ambiguous — did the node 'change'? The transaction emits a Deleted+Created pair of ChangeEvents, which IVM subscribers will process twice. Compare the thinness contract where updates to unchanged content should be no-ops.
findings.suggested_fix: Early-return fast path: `if old_cid == &new_node.cid()? { return Ok(old_cid.clone()); }` before entering the transaction. Also document the update semantics: 'If new_node hashes to old_cid, the call is a no-op that returns old_cid.' At minimum add a doctest demonstrating that update_node is idempotent on unchanged content.

findings.claim: `ErrorCodeStaticStr::as_str_owned_leaked` leaks on Unknown path; the match pattern is duplicated.
findings.detail: Lines 147-148 and lines 183 both match `ErrorCode::Unknown(_)` → `"E_UNKNOWN"`; the outer match arm at 147 literally guards against the arm at 183 that duplicates it. The comment at 138-140 says 'Leaks the string on the Unknown path' but the impl actually returns a static `"E_UNKNOWN"` for Unknown — nothing leaks. The docstring is misleading AND the outer match-then-inner-match dance is structurally unnecessary (the inner match covers every variant including Unknown, so the outer match is dead). Clippy's match_same_arms is allowed workspace-wide so this wasn't caught.
findings.suggested_fix: Drop the outer match wrapper entirely and just use the inner match directly on `self`. Update the docstring: no leak happens; the function returns a static slice. If the intent is to future-proof for Unknown carrying a user string, that requires a different return type (`Cow<'static, str>`) and the leak would need `Box::leak`.

findings.claim: ChangeProbe::drain ignores start_offset — not the contract the docstring implies.
findings.detail: The docstring at 1261-1262 says 'Events observed before the probe was created are not returned' and `start_offset` is captured at probe creation. But drain() on line 1265 does `let _ = self.start_offset;` explicitly discarding it, then calls `self.inner.drain_events()` which returns ALL currently-queued events. If probe A drains at offset 0 and probe B is created after 5 events and drains, probe B gets empty (A already drained). But if you create probe B FIRST and then commit 3 events, then create probe A at offset 3, then commit 2 more events: probe B drains 5, then probe A drains... 0, because B already drained. The semantics are 'first drain wins, events before probe creation are unfiltered' — not what the docstring claims.
findings.suggested_fix: Either (a) implement the documented semantics: store events in a ring buffer keyed by a monotonic counter so each probe filters events by `event_seq >= start_offset`, or (b) fix the docstring to match the actual 'queue-drain, call-once' semantics and rename `start_offset` to `_created_at_event_count` (or remove it entirely). The R3 tests claim ~35 are gated on primitive dispatch; it's worth verifying none of them exercise the multi-probe contract before landing (b).

findings.claim: IVM subscriber created fresh on every assemble() with no views, and Engine holds no handle to it — create_view cannot wire views into the actual subscriber.
findings.detail: Line 1020 constructs `benten_ivm::Subscriber::new()` and registers it as a change subscriber, but the Arc is moved into register_subscriber and not retained by Engine. When Engine::create_view is called, it writes a `system:IVMView` Node via the privileged put — but the in-flight Subscriber never has a `register_view` called on it. So `read_view(id)` for a view that was 'created' via `engine.create_view(...)` still returns UnknownView / IvmViewStale depending on whether id matches the hardcoded whitelist. This is acknowledged Phase-2 scope in the docstring at 1015-1018, but the gap between 'create_view succeeded' and 'read_view knows about it' is a correctness cliff users will stumble over.
findings.suggested_fix: Store the Subscriber Arc on Engine (as `Option<Arc<benten_ivm::Subscriber>>`). create_view should call `subscriber.register_view(def)` in addition to writing the system:IVMView Node. read_view_with should consult the subscriber for dynamic view ids, falling back to the hardcoded whitelist only for the 5 hand-written Phase-1 baselines. This closes the loop without requiring the Phase-2 primitive dispatch.

findings.claim: Single-file lib.rs at 1789 lines is at the upper bound of navigable; the G7 brief suggested splitting to builder.rs/engine.rs/wiring.rs.
findings.detail: The file is well-commented with prominent banner separators (lines 50/189/237/870/1060/1360/1620/1756 mark major sections) so it's genuinely navigable at its current size, but adding Engine::call's primitive-dispatch wiring in G6b and the napi-facing wrappers in G8 will push this past 2500 lines — past the point where PR diffs are reviewable. Current structure: errors, EngineInner, Engine impl (15 methods), EngineBuilder, supporting types (Outcome/Trace/AnchorHandle/ChangeProbe/...), EngineTransaction, SubgraphSpec/Builder, trait adapters, testing module, tests. Every one of these sections is a natural file boundary.
findings.suggested_fix: Before G8: split into `src/lib.rs` (re-exports + module wiring), `src/error.rs` (EngineError + ErrorCode bridge), `src/engine.rs` (Engine impl + CRUD), `src/builder.rs` (EngineBuilder), `src/transaction.rs` (EngineTransaction + NestedTx), `src/outcome.rs` (Outcome + Trace + TerminalError), `src/spec.rs` (SubgraphSpec + Builder + IterateBody + WriteSpec), `src/adapters.rs` (IntoSubgraphSpec / GrantSubject / RevokeSubject / RevokeScope / IntoCallInput). `src/testing.rs` for the testing module. This is a mechanical split with no logic change — safe to do in a follow-up commit between G7 and G8.

findings.claim: Both test-edit claims verified as surgical fixes, not contract weakening.
findings.detail: **compromise_4**: the original `content.contains("wasm32-wasip1")` matches the string anywhere in the workflow file. The comment above the test explicitly says the prohibition is on `cargo test --target wasm32-wasip1`, not `cargo build --target wasm32-wasip1` (determinism.yml legitimately uses the latter for T6 CID-canary builds). The fix narrows the grep to `line contains 'cargo test' AND line contains 'wasm32-wasip1'` on a per-line basis — tighter than the original in the sense that it matches the stated contract exactly, not looser. Risk: if a workflow has `cargo test` on one line and `wasm32-wasip1` on a different line, the per-line AND would miss it. Low risk given YAML style. **compromise_6**: adds `../../docs/SECURITY-POSTURE.md` as primary with `docs/SECURITY-POSTURE.md` as or_else fallback. Integration tests under `crates/benten-engine/tests/` run with CWD=crate-root (cargo test behavior), so `../../docs` resolves to the repo root docs/. This mirrors the existing pattern in compromise_4_wasm_runtime_is_phase_2's own path resolution. No contract change — still asserts `posture.contains("BLAKE3") && posture.contains("128")`.
findings.suggested_fix: Accept both test edits. Optional nit on compromise_4: the per-line AND could false-negative on an edge case — prefer `line.trim_start().starts_with("run:") && line.contains("cargo test") && line.contains("wasm32-wasip1")`, but this is over-engineering for a workflow-file guard.

findings.claim: ErrorCode enum duplicated here as a string-mapping table — drift risk against benten-core.
findings.detail: The big match at 149-183 enumerates every ErrorCode variant and maps it to a stable `&'static str`. benten-core presumably owns the canonical ErrorCode definition and each variant's stable code. If a new variant is added in benten-core (likely in G6b or G8), this match will fail to compile (exhaustive-match catches new-variant) — but ONLY if the enum is `#[non_exhaustive]` isn't in play. If anything weakens the exhaustive-match here, silently missing new codes will return 'E_UNKNOWN' without fanfare.
findings.suggested_fix: Move the ErrorCode → stable-string mapping INTO benten-core as an inherent method `ErrorCode::stable_code(&self) -> &'static str`. Delete the `ErrorCodeStaticStr` trait + match table here. benten-core is the single source of truth for the catalog, so centralizing the string conversion there eliminates drift risk and shortens lib.rs by ~40 lines. Matches the pattern CoreError/GraphError/CapError already use (their code() method returns the ErrorCode which then needs this mapping — circular layering).

findings.claim: benten-eval `diag` feature not enabled — handler_to_mermaid / handler_predecessors can never be wired.
findings.detail: Cargo.toml lines 14-20 list benten-eval as a plain path dep with no features. The G7 report flags handler_to_mermaid and handler_predecessors as 'diag-feature surface' deferred to Phase 2, which is consistent. No change needed now, but the Cargo.toml is missing a comment documenting that these stubs depend on enabling `benten-eval/diag` in a future Phase — easy to miss when G8 dispatches napi bindings that might want the diag paths.
findings.suggested_fix: Add a comment above line 17: `# benten-eval: enable the 'diag' feature in Phase 2 to wire handler_to_mermaid / handler_predecessors.` Or, if the diag feature already compiles in benten-eval today, enable it behind a benten-engine 'diag' feature flag and gate the two stub methods on that cfg.

findings.claim: SubgraphSpec::into_eval_subgraph fabricates a 'r_default' read handle when respond has no predecessor — risk of silent spec corruption.
findings.detail: Lines 1513-1515: `let prev = last.unwrap_or_else(|| sb.read("r_default"));` — if a SubgraphSpec has a respond primitive as the first operation (the builder allows this), we silently synthesize a READ named 'r_default' before the respond. This changes the handler's content-addressed CID in a way the caller did not request. A test that registers the same spec twice with the builder may get different CIDs if the builder's ordering varies. And the invariant validator then sees a handler with an extra READ node the user didn't write.
findings.suggested_fix: Error out instead: `let prev = last.ok_or(EngineError::Other { code: ErrorCode::InvRegistration, message: "respond primitive has no predecessor".into() })?;`. The SubgraphSpecBuilder can enforce this at build time by requiring at least one non-respond primitive before respond(). Alternatively, since the whole SubgraphSpec path is acknowledged as Phase-2 scope (the placeholder builder at 1416-1436 has empty write/iterate bodies), mark the `respond-without-predecessor` path as `unreachable!()` with a comment documenting that the Phase-2 builder will prevent it.

positive_observations: Pipeline is verifiably green: cargo fmt/clippy -D warnings/check/doc all clean.
Error handling is disciplined — only ONE .expect() in production code (line 842) and it's in a testing helper; all other production paths use `?` + typed errors.

EngineError variants are well-shaped: thiserror-derived, stable code() method, Box<RegistrationError> to respect the 128-byte result_large_err threshold (g6-cr-1 follow-through), and NotImplemented carries a diagnostic message string instead of a bare panic.
Mutex poisoning handled consistently via `.unwrap_or_else(|e| e.into_inner())` — no `.unwrap()` on lock() anywhere in production code.

Change-broadcast panic safety: ChangeBroadcast::publish wraps each subscriber callback in `std::panic::catch_unwind` so a buggy subscriber can't poison the publishing thread — tested by panicking_callback_does_not_poison_broadcast.

System-zone privileged API is genuinely minimal: create_node rejects `system:*` labels, grant/revoke/create_view/create_principal route through privileged_put_node + WriteContext::privileged_for_engine_api, making the privileged-write perimeter auditable.

Thinness honest-no contract is consistent: without_caps makes grant/revoke return SubsystemDisabled, without_ivm makes read_view return SubsystemDisabled, ivm_subscriber_count returns 0 when disabled. Reads always work.
The compromises_regression.rs edits are defensible, narrowly-scoped test-authoring bug fixes with clear before/after reasoning and mirror an existing in-file pattern.

Transaction's commit-time capability check (lines 698-744) is well-structured: user closure runs first, then capability check on collected PendingOps before the redb commit fires. Panic safety inherited from G3-A. A cap-denial correctly aborts the transaction via GraphError::TxAborted and surfaces as EngineError::Cap in the saved user_result.
notes:
G7 is a solid foundation that I'd call accept-with-minors rather than pass only because (a) the production()+without_caps() footgun is a real inconsistency that will bite the first ops engineer who finds it, and (b) the Box<dyn CapabilityPolicy> in the public builder will force a G8 shim. Neither is a blocker for R5 advancement — both are reasonable to address in a follow-up commit before G8 dispatches. The remaining 11 minors are paper-cuts (missing docs, single-file navigability, ChangeProbe start_offset dead code, update_node no-op optimization, ErrorCode mapping drift risk) that I'd batch into a tidy-up pass. Test edits are genuinely surgical; no contract weakening detected. Recommend advancing to G6b/G8 dispatch gating with the two majors triaged back to G7 as must-fix and the minors deferred to a cleanup sweep.

G7 scope boundary validated: the 35 tests gated on Engine::call primitive dispatch, the 4 on SnapshotHandle::scan_label, and the 2 on benten-eval aggregate-mode validation are all legitimately external to G7's charter.
